Part 3: Stage-by-Stage Script Library

Source: David Duford — Final Expense Telesales Script (davidduford.com)

This section reproduces the structural script that Duford teaches agents to follow. The AI should feed these lines to the human agent as suggestions, adapting placeholders ([name], [state], [company]) to the live call.

Stage 1: Opening & Rapport

Opener: "[Mrs. Prospect], this is [Agent Name] from [Agency]. I'm reaching out in reference to an inquiry you made about our final expense program. First and foremost, I'm a licensed insurance agent in your state, and I'm local to you, living in [City]. What I do is help people 50 and older get affordable final expense coverage to pay their burial and cremation costs."

Warm-up: "But before we get into any of that, please tell me, how are you doing today? Do you have kids that live close by? Are they able to help out?"

Rapport questions: "Did you grow up in [City]? How long have you lived there? What did you retire from? When you're not locked up in the house, what do you do for fun?"

AI NOTE: Rapport matters more on the phone than face-to-face. Allow 3–5 minutes for warm-up. If the prospect laughs or becomes relaxed, prompt the agent to transition. If the prospect is stiff after 5 minutes, transition anyway — forcing rapport backfires.

Stage 2: Introduction / Transition

Transition: "So now you've told me a little about yourself, let me tell you about who I am and what I do. As I said earlier, my name is [Agent Name]. I'm an independent, licensed life insurance agent in your state, and my license number is [Number]. I've been in the business since [Year] and specialize in helping people like you get an affordable final expense program to cover burial and cremation costs."

Broker statement: "The reason my clients do business with me is because I'm what's known as a broker. Do you know what that is? Basically, I'm not married to one company like American General or State Farm. Instead, I have access to all of the companies — I shop all the companies to get you not only the best price, but the best value of coverage. Basically, I get my clients the best bang for their buck."

AI NOTE: Verify the agent says "independent" or "broker." Captive positioning kills shoppers. Flag if missed.

Stage 3: Fact-Finding

Opening question: "Like I said earlier, the reason people request information is because they're concerned about the high costs of final expenses and want a program to take care of it, so their kids don't come out of pocket for it. So tell me, what were your thoughts and concerns when you requested this information?"

Probing follow-up: "Tell me more."

Deeper probe: "When you say you were "curious," how do you mean?"

Emotional leverage: "What experiences have you had dealing with funerals in your family?"

If a story surfaces about a family member without coverage, the AI should prompt these deeper questions:

Agent: "So how did the family handle [brother's] burial without him having insurance?"

Agent: "Tell me how it made you feel when you found out [brother] wasn't insured and that you and your family were burdened with the burial cost?"

Agent: "How would things have been different if [brother] even had half of what y'all spent on the burial?"

The 3 Reasons Script

If the prospect gives vague answers ("I was just curious," "Just wanted to see what it was about"), the AI should surface this script:

Agent: "Mr. Jones, when I visit with people who send in this card, typically they're in one of three situations. The first is they know that if they die today, there's no coverage in place, and a loved one will end up paying the bill, and they don't want that. Second situation is they have some coverage, but not enough, due to increasing costs, or maybe they're paying too much for existing coverage. Third situation is they have plenty, but want to leave extra behind to a loved one. Out of those three — 1, not having any and wanting coverage, 2, having some and wanting more or a lower price, and 3, wanting to leave extra behind — where do you fit best?"

Existing Coverage Discovery

Agent: "Who do you have your existing life insurance coverage with? Great — and what's the name of the company? How much coverage? How much a month? Is it whole or term life? Do you know if it cancels at 80? Why don't you grab the policy for me so I can compare what you have to what I do."

Devil's Advocate Question

Use when the prospect seems low-quality or claims to already have plenty of coverage:

Agent: "Mrs. Jones, what I'm hearing is you have a ton of insurance and have no problems whatsoever paying for your burial costs. At $25,000 in coverage, you have way more than the average person and more than enough to do what's required. Tell me, based on that, what's the real reason to have any more than you have now?"

Summarizing Before Pre-Qualification

Agent: "So [Mrs. Prospect], let me make sure I understand everything. What you're looking for is enough coverage to pay for a burial, because you don't want to burden your relatives like your brother did, correct? And you're not looking for too much coverage, because you don't want to make your kids rich, and you want to make sure you have a fairly priced program too, is that right?"

AI NOTE: Always surface a summary script before transitioning to health questions. The summary creates commitment — the prospect has now verbally agreed to WHY they want coverage. Their own words will be referenced later during objection handling.

Stage 4: Pre-Qualification

Transition: "OK, Mr. Jones — now that I know what your particular situation is, let me go ahead and ask you some basic health questions, nothing too specific, but enough to figure out which program would be the best."

The required health questions in order:

What is your birth date?

Do you use tobacco in any form in the past 12 months? What kind?

Do you have any lung problems, kidney problems, or liver problems? COPD, emphysema, oxygen tank usage, hepatitis, or dialysis?

Any diabetes? Pills or insulin? Diabetic complications?

Any history of heart problems? Heart attacks, strokes, seizures, bypasses, stents, pacemakers? Take nitroglycerin?

Have you been hospitalized overnight in the past 12 months for any reason?

Any cancer ever?

Any depression, bipolar, schizophrenia, dementia, Alzheimer's? Any neurological problems like lupus or Parkinson's?

What's your height and weight?

Can you grab your medications for me and spell them out?

Payment Method

Agent: "Two more questions. First, as far as billing works, since the company doesn't send me out to collect the money like in the old days, they require everyone paying monthly to set up either a draft from their checking, savings, or debit card account. Which do you use?"

Budget Anchoring

Agent: "My final question is about price. Let me be totally upfront with you. Nine out of ten people I see draw disability or are retired. Everyone I see is on a fixed income. They all want life insurance, but they want to make sure they can afford it. What's the point of having a policy that's too much, right, that you have to drop in six months? The truth is, the best policy to have is the one that's there when you die. And the one that's there when you die is the one you can comfortably afford. If I can qualify you for a program today, can you afford somewhere between $150 and $200 a month?"

AI NOTE: Never skip the budget question. Without a confirmed budget, any quote is a shot in the dark. Keep stepping down until the prospect commits.

Stage 5: Presentation

The presentation teaches the difference between three product types, then positions final expense whole life as the superior choice.

Term Life Education (using AARP/Globe as examples)

Agent: "Do you know the difference between term and whole life insurance? Most people don't, so let me explain. You know who AARP is, right? They offer a life insurance program called a "Level Term" product. The simple way to remember how term works is that all term TERMINATES. It cancels. AARP and Globe are designed to cancel your coverage at age 80. And it gets worse — they actually increase your premiums over time. They start you cheap, then raise the price when you're retired and your budget is fixed, in order to get rid of you so they don't have to pay the claim."

Closing the loop: "Are you planning on kicking the bucket before or after 80? [Prospect says after or doesn't know.] Exactly — who on Earth knows when our final day's going to be? So what's the point of having a policy that cancels out before you need it?"

Guaranteed Issue Education (using Colonial Penn as example)

Agent: "Have you heard about Colonial Penn with the Jeopardy guy? They do what's called "Guaranteed Acceptance" coverage. They don't ask you health questions. Sounds a little fishy, huh? Let me read the fine print: there is a reduced death benefit for two years if death occurs by non-accidental death. What that really means is you have no natural-death coverage for the first two years. This kind of insurance is perfect for people bedridden or in horrible health who won't qualify anywhere else."

Final Expense Whole Life Presentation

Agent: "This leads me to what I do. I offer Final Expense Whole Life Insurance, and there are three main benefits. First, your coverage is Day 1, 100% coverage from the first day. You're completely covered when you start, no two-year waiting period. Second, your coverage never cancels due to age or health. Third, your price is guaranteed to never increase. You choose the beneficiary, and the death benefit is tax-free to them."

The Three-Way Close

Agent: "So [Mrs. Prospect], out of all three programs you saw — the term that cancels and has rate increases, the Colonial Penn policies that make you wait two years, or mine that has immediate 100% coverage with fixed rates guaranteed never to increase — which program fits your needs best? And what do you like the best about my program versus the others?"

Stage 6: Close & Cool-Down

Cool-down recap: "To wrap up, we've taken out a policy for [$12,000] in coverage that's Day 1, 100% coverage. The rate is [$50] a month, and that rate never increases. And you cannot be cancelled due to age or health. My name and phone number are here — please feel comfortable calling me with any questions or concerns. I'm your service agent going forward."

Lock-in questions: "At this point, do you have any questions? How do you feel about your coverage? Is the price definitely comfortable for you? Is there any reason at this point you would not keep your coverage?"

CRITICAL: After the close, talk about ANYTHING OTHER THAN INSURANCE for 3–5 minutes. This lock-in conversation prevents cancellations. The AI should remind the agent to do this and may suggest topics based on rapport-stage data (hobbies, grandkids, hometown).

Part 4: The 5 Barriers Framework

Source: Life Insurance Academy — Chris Ball and Roger Short

Life Insurance Academy teaches that every objection, no matter how it's worded, traces back to one of five underlying barriers. "I want to think about it" is almost never the real reason — it's a smokescreen for one of these five. The AI must diagnose which barrier is active and suggest the matching response.

The Five Barriers

Barrier 1: No Trust

Signals: prospect questions the agent's credentials, company name, or the offer itself. Vague skepticism. "How do I know this is real?"

Response strategy: build trust through authority + empathy. State license, years of experience, share a personal story. Reference the prospect back to something they already said that indicated they were open.

Barrier 2: No Need

Signals: "I don't really need this." "I already have enough." "My kids can handle it."

Response strategy: need cannot be CREATED — only DISCOVERED. Ask probing questions to uncover a latent need. "What would happen if you passed tomorrow and the burial cost $12,000? Who pays that?"

Barrier 3: No Want

Signals: the prospect acknowledges a need but isn't emotionally engaged. "Yeah, I probably should get something." "One of these days."

Response strategy: use the 4 STAKES questions to move from "need" to "want":

Who depends on you financially?

What specifically do you want to leave them?

What would happen to them if you didn't get this done?

How would you feel knowing you could have prevented that?

Barrier 4: No Urgency

Signals: "I'll get to it." "I need to think about it." "Call me next month."

Response strategy: create urgency through health risk framing. "Your health today qualifies you for [rate]. If anything changes — a blood pressure medication, a diagnosis — that rate goes up or coverage becomes impossible. The best day to lock in is always today."

Barrier 5: No Money

Signals: "I can't afford it." "Money is tight." "Not in my budget."

Response strategy: handle affordability with dignity. Never talk down. Step down the coverage amount to fit the budget. "Something is better than nothing." Refer to Duford's step-down scripts.

Tie-Downs: The Secret to Closing

Life Insurance Academy teaches "tie-downs" (micro-agreements) as the secret sauce of consistent closes. Small, intentional yeses throughout the presentation create momentum and uncover misunderstandings early.

Examples of tie-downs the AI should prompt:

"That makes sense, doesn't it?"

"You wouldn't want your family to deal with that, would you?"

"It's better to have it and not need it than need it and not have it, right?"

"That's the kind of protection you want, isn't it?"

"If we can make the price work, that's what you'd want to do, correct?"

The AI should track tie-down count per presentation. Top producers hit 5–8 tie-downs before the close.

The 3 C's of Closing

LIA Podcast: Roger Short and Zach

Clarity — the prospect must understand exactly what they're buying, how much it costs, and what it does.

Confidence — the agent must sound certain. Hesitation transfers to the prospect.

Connection — the relationship built in Stage 1 carries the sale in Stage 6.

The AI should flag agent hesitation, filler words, and drops in vocal energy in real time.

Five Reasons Agents Fail in Telesales

Script isn't mastered — sounds robotic, agent gets thrown off by deviations.

Confidence is low — the agent isn't certain about products or process.

Medications and underwriting basics aren't understood.

Affordability isn't discovered before pricing — agent quotes blind.

Agent hesitates at the close — doesn't confidently move to the application.

The AI should monitor for these failure patterns and coach accordingly.

Part 5: Cody Askins Cold Calling & Follow-Up System

Source: Cody Askins — The Power Players Podcast, CodyAskins.com, EverQuote Webinar

Cody Askins is known in the industry for aggressive prospecting cadences and a systematic approach to lead conversion. His core insights for a voice AI:

The Follow-Up Problem

92% of sales professionals fail to make it in the insurance industry. A primary cause: inadequate follow-up. About 80% of lead conversions happen between the 5th and 12th contact attempt, yet more than 50% of sales professionals don't make it to a second attempt.

AI MANDATE: The AI must aggressively recommend follow-up when a prospect doesn't close on the first call. Track contact attempts and surface reminders at days 1, 3, 7, 14, 30, 60, 90.

The Triple Dial Technique

The traditional double-dial: call, hang up if no answer, wait 30 seconds, call again. Askins adds a third dial, with no wait between — three back-to-back calls. The logic: modern phone users ignore a single missed call but answer or at least notice a rapid three-call pattern.

The AI should remind the agent to triple-dial after an initial no-answer.

The 12-Step First 72 Hours System

Askins teaches a specific 12-step cadence for the first 72 hours after receiving a lead. The core principle: speed and multi-channel contact. Key steps the AI should enforce:

Contact within 5 minutes of lead receipt (conversion rates drop sharply after this window).

Use phone, text, and email in parallel — not sequentially.

Never leave a dead lead unchecked for more than 24 hours in the first three days.

Re-engagement messaging should vary in tone, not repeat.

The "Money Bag" Cross-Sell Technique

Source: CodyAskins.com/tag/script

Askins frames every call as an opportunity to build a "money bag" — a bundle of products the client needs together (life + accident + supplemental, for example). The AI should listen for cross-sell signals:

Mention of spouse or children without coverage

Mention of chronic health condition (indicates critical illness opportunity)

Mention of retirement concerns (indicates annuity or IUL opportunity)

Mention of grandchildren (indicates legacy/trust opportunity)

10-Benefit Discovery Method

Askins teaches agents to list and review at least 10 benefits of their product during the presentation, watching for which benefits the prospect reacts to most strongly. Those reactions become the pivot points for the close.

The AI should maintain a benefit-interest map during the call, noting verbal and prosodic reactions (pauses, "oh really," energy changes) to specific benefits. At close time, it surfaces the top 2–3 most-engaged benefits back to the agent.

The Trial Close

Askins teaches a low-pressure trial close that sounds like a natural continuation of the conversation rather than a close:

Trial close: "Based on what we've talked about, it sounds like [plan] would be the best fit for you. If we can get you approved today, how soon would you want the coverage to start?"

The AI should prompt trial closes at natural pause points, not at the end of the presentation. If the prospect answers the trial close, they've effectively closed themselves.

Part 6: Summit Life Group Telesales Fundamentals

Source: Summit Life Group — slgteam.com (The Lifestyle IMO)

Summit Life Group specializes in tele-sales life insurance agency support, providing training, high-level carrier contracts, exclusive leads, and one-on-one coaching from top producers. Their core teaching principles for a voice AI:

The Lifestyle IMO Philosophy

Summit emphasizes that telesales life insurance is a lifestyle business — not a grind. Successful agents build systems that allow them to produce consistently without burning out. For a voice AI, this means supporting sustainable agent behavior, not maximum-pressure selling.

Core Telesales Workflow Principles

1. Exclusive Leads Discipline

Agents should work leads systematically, not at random. The AI should track which leads have been contacted, how many times, and with what result. Never re-contact a dead lead without a specific new angle.

2. Carrier Selection as Closing Tool

Because Summit-supported agents have access to multiple top carriers, matching the right carrier to the right prospect is part of the sale. The AI should note which carriers match the prospect's health profile in real time, so the agent isn't scrambling at the quote stage.

3. One-on-One Coaching Feedback

Summit's model emphasizes ongoing coaching from top producers. A voice AI replicates this coaching loop: each call generates data (stage timing, objection count, tie-down count, close result) that feeds into agent development.

Telesales-Specific Tactical Principles

Don't leave long pauses. Phone silence feels 2x longer to the prospect than to the agent.

Match the prospect's pace. Older prospects speak slower — the agent must slow down.

Avoid jargon. "Underwriting," "guaranteed issue," "graded death benefit" all need plain-language translation until the presentation stage.

Use the prospect's name 4–6 times over a 20–30 minute call — often enough to personalize, not so often it sounds scripted.

Never share a quote before affordability is confirmed.

Ask one question at a time. Phone prospects can't handle multi-part questions the way they can in person.

AI NOTE: The voice AI should detect pace mismatch between agent and prospect (words-per-minute delta) and prompt the agent to slow down. This is one of the most common telesales failures.

Part 7: Real-Time Objection Detection & Rebuttal Map

Each row below is a pattern for the voice AI to match in real time. The AI listens for the trigger language, tags the barrier type, and surfaces the suggested rebuttal to the agent.

Objection: "I need to think about it"

Barrier: Usually Barrier 1 (Trust) or Barrier 3 (No Want) — rarely the real reason.

Suggested rebuttal: "That's fine, [Mrs. Prospect]. And when you say you need to think about it, how do you mean?"

After they answer, isolate: "Besides [their stated concern], is there any other reason you would not move forward today?"

Conditional close: "If I can [solve that concern], is there any reason you would not move forward today?"

Objection: "I can't afford it"

Barrier: Barrier 5 (No Money) — or sometimes Barrier 3 (No Want) in disguise.

Suggested rebuttal: "That's fine, [Mr. Prospect]. Affordability is really important. I've got a family and bills to pay too, so I totally get it. My question is this: is there any other issue besides affordability that would cause you not to do business today?"

Step-down close: "If we can find something affordable that satisfies what you're looking to do, can we move forward? Good — let me show you a smaller plan. Something is better than nothing, and your [child/spouse] would rather you have some coverage than none at all, right?"

Objection: "I don't need it / I already have coverage"

Barrier: Barrier 2 (No Need).

Suggested rebuttal: "That's fine, [Mr. Prospect]. And when you say you don't think you need this, how do you mean?"

Reference their own words: "Correct me if I'm wrong, but earlier you said [quote from Stage 3]. You said that without coverage, your [daughter/son] would be in trouble. NOT paying the premium does not solve that problem. Taking out coverage today for a need you already said you have IS the solution."

Objection: "I need to talk to my spouse/kids"

Barrier: Barrier 1 (Trust) or stalling tactic.

Suggested rebuttal: "I think we both agree that your [children] would prefer you having coverage over not, right? How would you feel if your [children] had to pull $12,000 out of nowhere to pay for your funeral expenses? Let's get you qualified with this plan, and you can show them the plan after the fact. I'm available to answer any of their questions."

Objection: "Just mail me something"

Barrier: Barrier 1 (Trust).

Suggested rebuttal: "I'm not sure what I would mail you — that's the purpose of this call. I still need to determine which plans and options you best qualify for. It only takes a few minutes to go over that. Are you a morning or afternoon person? 9 or 11?"

Objection: "I don't remember requesting this"

Barrier: Barrier 1 (Trust) or genuine confusion.

Suggested rebuttal: "I completely understand — sometimes requests come from past inquiries. Let me verify: is your address still [address]? Great. The reason I'm calling is because I help people your age get affordable final expense coverage so their families don't come out of pocket for burial costs. Is that something you'd want to hear more about, even for just a minute?"

Objection: "I'm happy with my current provider"

Barrier: Barrier 4 (No Urgency) or genuine satisfaction.

Suggested rebuttal: "That's great — it's smart to have coverage you trust. Out of curiosity, when was the last time your policy was reviewed? If I could find another A-rated company with the same coverage at a lower rate, would it be worth 5 minutes to compare?"

Objection: "I'm too busy"

Barrier: Barrier 4 (No Urgency) or genuine schedule issue.

Suggested rebuttal: "Completely understand. How about we schedule a specific time that's convenient? Just 10 minutes, and I'll have your quote ready. Would evening work better, or is weekends easier for you?"

Objection: "This is too expensive"

Barrier: Barrier 5 (No Money) — but often really Barrier 3 (No Want).

Suggested rebuttal: "I hear you. Let me ask — is it that the dollar amount is outside your budget, or is it that you're not sure the coverage is worth that amount? [Listen to the answer.] If affordability, step down to a smaller plan. If value, re-sell the benefits using their own stated reasons."

Objection: "I want to research online"

Barrier: Barrier 1 (Trust).

Suggested rebuttal: "I encourage you to feel fully informed. Are there specific questions I can help with right now? Because here's the thing — every quote online is based on a generic health profile. What I can get you is a quote based on your specific situation, and that's usually better than anything online."

Closing Principles for the Voice AI

The underlying philosophy across all four source trainers — Duford, Askins, Life Insurance Academy, and Summit Life Group — converges on these truths:

1. The Prospect's Own Words Are the Most Powerful Tool

Capture everything they say in Stage 3. Use it verbatim in Stage 5 (objection handling) and Stage 6 (close). The prospect cannot argue with their own reasons.

2. Objections Are Not Rejections

Every objection is an unanswered question or an unmet emotional need. The agent's job is to diagnose the barrier, not to "overcome" resistance. The AI's job is to diagnose faster than the agent can on their own.

3. The First Objection Is Rarely the Real One

"I need to think about it" is a smokescreen about 80% of the time. The AI must always suggest probing ("when you say that, how do you mean?") before suggesting a rebuttal.

4. Budget Must Be Confirmed Before Quoting

Quoting before the prospect agrees to a range is the single most common failure pattern. The AI should refuse to allow the agent to quote until a budget range is verbally confirmed.

5. Follow-Up Is Where the Money Lives

Most sales happen between contacts 5 and 12. The AI must enforce the follow-up cadence or recommend re-assignment of the lead.

6. The Cool-Down Prevents Chargebacks

3–5 minutes of non-insurance conversation after the close cements the sale emotionally. The AI should never let the agent hang up immediately after the application is signed.

7. The Voice AI Is a Copilot, Not a Pilot

The human agent remains in control. The AI's role is to surface relevant information at the right moment, not to take over the conversation. The best AI-agent dynamic is whisper suggestions that the human decides whether to use.

Key Concepts and Insights: Becoming a Recession-Proof Sales Agent

All selling is about change: The core of sales is helping prospects change their current situation, moving away from pain or risk towards a better, safer outcome. However, human beings inherently resist change because it triggers discomfort and uncertainty.

Selling results, not products: Agents are not selling insurance policies or products per se; they are selling the results and peace of mind those policies provide—such as financial security for a family after a loss.

Three critical steps to recession-proof sales success:

Become a Problem Finder and Problem Solver – Instead of pushing products, agents must discover hidden or unrecognized problems prospects have and demonstrate how their solutions solve those problems.

Ask the Right Questions at the Right Time – Timing, tone, and phrasing of questions are crucial to uncovering prospect needs and avoiding triggering resistance.

Eliminate Sales Resistance – Neutralize hidden sales pressure early in conversations to build trust and open prospects up.

Old sales techniques are obsolete: Jeremy recounts his early sales career selling door-to-door alarm systems, where enthusiasm and feature pitches led to repeated objections and failures. He realized traditional "pushy" sales scripts alienate modern prospects.

Behavioral Science and Neuroscience in Selling:

Human decision-making is primarily emotional, not logical.

The most persuasive communication is dialogue-based, where prospects persuade themselves via neuro-emotional persuasion questions (NPQ).

Aggressive or assumptive sales tactics trigger the brain’s fight-or-flight response, causing prospects to shut down.

Sales communication modes:

Era 1: Hard Selling (least effective) – Dominating, pushing features/benefits, assuming the sale.

Era 2: Consultative Selling – Asking logical needs-based questions, but often surface-level and insufficient because prospects don’t fully know their needs.

Era 3: Dialogue & Neuro-Emotional Persuasion (most effective) – Asking skilled emotional questions that help prospects uncover and own their problems.

Common Sales Mistakes Highlighted

Product pushing and feature dumping: Focusing on the product instead of prospect problems leads to resistance and objections.

Asking premature or assumptive questions: Budget or coverage questions too early cause prospects to shut down.

Using scripted pitches and enthusiasm without empathy: Prospects see through forced excitement and generic claims like “best coverage” or “lowest price.”

Triggering sales resistance by appearing needy or pushy: Needing the sale or acting desperate activates fight-or-flight responses.

Ignoring the emotional state of prospects: Logical appeals alone are insufficient; emotional engagement is key.

Psychological and Communication Insights

Prospects subconsciously pick up verbal and non-verbal cues within the first 7-12 seconds of interaction.

Agents must adopt a neutral, detached tone (referred to as "collective confidence") to promote curiosity and openness.

Using phrases like “I’m not sure you should yet” or “Maybe you don’t even need us” disarms resistance by removing pressure.

Building a gap between the prospect’s current state and desired state (emphasizing risk of not changing) creates urgency.

How to Ask BETTER Sales Questions

Common sales questions are often ineffective, such as:

"What's your biggest challenge?"

"What are two problems that keep you awake at night?"

"What's your biggest need right now?"

These questions are too generic and familiar, causing prospects to give guarded or superficial answers.

Prospects perceive generic questions as low-value and salesy, which lowers the salesperson’s status and trustworthiness.

To gain deeper insights, salespeople must ask tailored, skeptical, and paced questions that prompt prospects to explain and justify their current situation or dissatisfaction.

Verbal pacing (strategic pauses within questions) encourages prospects to think more deeply and respond thoughtfully.

When prospects verbalize their own problems and reasons for change, they are self-persuading, which is far more effective than the salesperson telling them what to think.

The emotional drivers behind a prospect’s decision to change are primarily:

Pain from the current situation

Fear of future pain or loss

Without evoking these drivers, prospects have little motivation to buy or change.

Psychology Hacks To Close More Sales

Key Insights

Belief systems are ingrained from childhood and heavily influence sales conversations. These frames act as mental barriers or defensive mechanisms when prospects feel they are being sold to.

Common sales objections stem from these belief systems, such as “how much is this going to cost?” or “I need to talk to my spouse.” These are not just surface objections but deep-rooted beliefs influenced by society and prior experiences.

The goal in sales is to D-frame the prospect, meaning to remove them from their current limiting belief system. Without doing this, typical sales objections will block the sale.

Reframing is essential to align the prospect’s priorities with your solution. For example, switching their mindset from “I can’t afford this” to “this solution is now my number one priority.”

Every question asked during the sales process must serve one or more of these functions:

Trigger doubt about the prospect’s current situation or solution.

Prevent objections by addressing potential concerns early (preframing).

Help the prospect feel the pain of their current problem and the fear of continuing pain if unchanged.

Enable the prospect to visualize a positive future once the problem is solved (future pacing).

Example of preframing objection about spouse approval:

Asking a prospect (e.g., a real estate agent named Dan) how his spouse feels about his current low listings, linking the spouse’s desire for more income to the benefit of the offered solution.

Visualization and emotional engagement:

Asking prospects what they would do with extra income or benefits creates a logical and emotional connection, deepening their desire to buy.

Sales Question Framework

Questions must:

Seed doubt about current circumstances or solutions.

Prevent objections before they occur.
